sentences.base_language: I am lost and I need to find the metro station.
sentences.target_language: أنا ضائع وأحتاج إلى إيجاد محطة المترو.
sentences.romanized: ana da'i' wa-ahtaj ila ijad mahatta al-mitru.
sentences.literal: I lost and-I-need to finding station the-metro.
sentences.grammarNote: ضَائِع (ḍāʾiʿ, lost) — فَاعِل active participle from root ض-ي-ع (ḍ-y-ʿ, to be lost/wasted). أَحتَاج إِلى (aḥtāj ilā, I need) — Form VIII of ح-و-ج (ḥ-w-j, need). Note: احتاج always takes إلى (not direct object): أَحتَاج إِلى كذا = I need such-and-such. إِيجَاد (ījād, finding — Form IV verbal noun of و-ج-د, to find).

sentences.base_language: I stop a man on the street.
sentences.grammarNote: أُوقِف (awqif, I stop) — Form IV of root و-ق-ف (w-q-f, to stand/stop): وَقَفَ (to stand) → أَوقَفَ (to stop/make stand — causative). رَجُلاً (rajulan, a man) — indefinite accusative (tanwin ـاً) as direct object. The sun letter ش in الشَّارع (the street) causes assimilation: ash-shāriʿ.

sentences.base_language: Excuse me, where is the nearest metro station?
sentences.grammarNote: أقرب (aqrab) = nearest, the superlative of قريب (qarib, near). Arabic superlatives are formed on the pattern أَفعَل (af'al): قريب → أقرب, بعيد → أبعد, كبير → أكبر. This pattern covers most common adjectives.

sentences.base_language: Of course! Go straight along this street.
sentences.target_language: بالتأكيد! اذهب مباشرة على هذا الشارع.
sentences.literal: With-the-certainty! Go straight on this the-street.
sentences.grammarNote: اِذهَب (idhhab, go! — imperative of ذ-ه-ب, to go). مُبَاشَرةً (mubāsharatan, straight/directly) — Form III verbal noun used as an adverb (accusative). بِالتَّأكِيد (bit-taʾkīd, certainly) = by confirmation — from أَكَّدَ (to confirm, Form II) from أ-ك-د. هَذَا الشَّارِع (hādhā ash-shāriʿ) = this street — demonstrative + definite noun.

sentences.base_language: Then turn left at the big mosque.
sentences.target_language: ثم التفِ يساراً عند المسجد الكبير.
sentences.literal: Then turn(you) left at the-mosque the-big.
sentences.grammarNote: اِلتَفِ (iltafi, turn! — Form VIII imperative of ل-ف-ف, to wind/turn): اِلتَفَّ (iltaffa, to turn/wrap around). يَسَاراً (yasāran, to the left — accusative adverb): يَسَار (left) from root ي-س-ر (ease/left). المَسجِد الكَبِير (al-masjid al-kabīr, the big mosque) — definite noun + definite adjective. مَسجِد from root س-ج-د (to prostrate in prayer).

sentences.base_language: The station is about two hundred metres from the mosque.
sentences.target_language: المحطة على بُعد مئتي متر من المسجد.
sentences.romanized: al-mahatta 'ala bu'd mi'atayn mitr min al-masjid.
sentences.literal: The-station at distance two-hundred metre from the-mosque.
sentences.grammarNote: مئتي (mi'atayn) is the dual of مئة (mi'a, hundred). For numbers, Arabic uses the dual form for exactly two hundred. The number comes before the unit: مئتي متر = two hundred metres.

sentences.base_language: You will see it on your right, near the pharmacy.
sentences.target_language: سترى المحطة على يمينك، بجانب الصيدلية.
sentences.literal: Will-see(you) the-station on your-right, beside the-pharmacy.
sentences.grammarNote: سَتَرَى (satarā, you will see) — سـ (sa-, near future prefix) + تَرَى (you see, defective verb from ر-أ-ي). The hamza drops in conjugation: يَرَى (he sees), تَرَى (you see). يَمِينِك (yamīnik, your right) — يَمِين (right) + possessive suffix ـك (your). الصَّيدَلِيَّة (aṣ-ṣaydaliyya, pharmacy) — sun letter ص causes assimilation.

هِي (hiya, she/it — feminine pronoun) refers to المَحَطَّة (the station, feminine). Arabic pronouns must agree with the grammatical gender of the noun they replace. بَعِيدَة (baʿīda, far, feminine) agrees with المَحَطَّة. Compare: هُو (huwa, he/it — masculine) for masculine nouns. هُنَا (hunā, here) vs. هُنَاك (hunāk, there).

sentences.base_language: No, it is very close — about five minutes on foot.
sentences.target_language: لا، هي قريبة جداً — حوالي خمس دقائق مشياً.
sentences.romanized: la, hiya qariba jiddan — hawali khams daqa'iq mashyan.
sentences.literal: No, it(f.) close very — about five minutes walking.
sentences.grammarNote: حَوَالَي (ḥawālī, approximately/about) — from root ح-و-ل (ḥ-w-l, to surround/change): 'in the area of.' دَقَائِق (daqāʾiq, minutes) — broken plural of دَقِيقَة (daqīqa, minute/precise): pattern فَعَائِل. مَشياً (mashyan, on foot/walking) — indefinite accusative of مَشي (walking), used as an adverb of manner (ḥāl): 'while walking.'

sentences.base_language: Thank you very much, you are very kind.
sentences.grammarNote: شُكراً جَزِيلاً (shukran jazīlan) = thank you very much — both words are in the accusative indefinite (tanwin ـاً), functioning as an exclamatory expression. شُكراً is the verbal noun of شَكَرَ (to thank); جَزِيل (plentiful/abundant) modifies it. لَطِيف (laṭīf, kind/gentle) from root ل-ط-ف (l-ṭ-f, to be gentle): لُطف (kindness), لَطِيف is also a name of God (the Subtle One).
